chunk_summary: Albuminuria and hyperuricemia screening are critical in severe pediatric obesity, especially with hypertension or metabolic syndrome, to assess kidney risk and guide renoprotective therapy and lifestyle interventions.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of albuminuria and hyperuricemia as markers of kidney risk in severe pediatric obesity.
Learn practical screening methods, including ACR and serum creatinine, for early detection of renal complications.
Explore lifestyle interventions and referral pathways for managing obesity-related kidney risks and hyperuricemia.
Recognize when to involve pediatric nephrology for renoprotective therapy in severe obesity cases.
key_facts: Albuminuria screening involves a first-morning urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (ACR) or protein-to-creatinine ratio; persistent ACR ≥30 mg/g warrants nephrology referral.
Hyperuricemia clusters with insulin resistance and hypertension in adolescents with obesity; routine uric acid testing is reasonable in severe cases with metabolic syndrome.
Weight management is central to addressing obesity-related glomerulopathy and hyperuricemia.
Renal risk screening is recommended in severe obesity with hypertension, dysglycemia, or a family history of kidney disease.
Lifestyle changes, such as reducing sugar-sweetened beverages and improving activity, are first-line management for hyperuricemia.
reference_quote: Kidney risk is under-recognized in pediatric obesity because early disease can be silent. Obesity-related hyperfiltration and glomerulopathy can present initially with proteinuria or albuminuria before serum creatinine becomes abnormal.
document_key_topics: Renal risk assessment and nephrology referral
